comments: Miguel Diaz - 03/07/23  6:40:16 AM: Max was present and looked the  fire alarm panel and found no alarms that led him to the TPF flame ex panel that was ringing he used the guide that is located on top of the flame ex panel to silence the alarm using code is 3333 .I was called in when I arrived I looked for the heat source and found the 1080 drilling machine with a really bad dull drill bit this machine is what caused the alarm fire department captain agreed so did ME .Suggest operators get trained on what to look for  that tells you when to replace the drill smoke is one of them another is  if the hole has a flare up that means the drill is not drilling smoothly anymore lastly if the bottom of the hole is blown out that's another alarm yesterday they had all three signs .  .Fire department was onsite they helped by using their infrared device they found no heat source in our ducting they also verified that the sprinkler head was not used in the dust collector .We did have running water from the flame ex valve that was stuck open .Used scissor lift  to go up  ibn machine shop  and turn the handle to shut the water off .Removed four bolts on the flame ex solenoid valve and removed the diaphragm found it dirty  i cleaned it and open the small pin hole that was dirty after opening the hole i assembled it back and open the valve that stopped the water from coming out .Side note if this does not stop the Water from coming out try wiping the infrared sensors in the ducting free of water if they have water in front of the lens  this will also cause the system to keep the water going .Lastly we checked the filters and found them in good shape some were damped we removed all 12 doors left it open overnight so they can dry up and this morning i turned the system on and let it run in cleaning mode this pushed air out in each rack drying them up more .Dust collector numbers are normal and production is running .Flame ex tech support (336)299-2933 flame JM Carden fire tech support 9951)852-9972 .

problem:
Issue:
• The leveling bar drilling machine was reported to have been drilling with chipping on the bottom side (see Bottom Side Chip attachment).
o Chips large enough to be a quality issue that won’t be covered by the leveling bars.
comments:
Eric Ramirez - 08/13/25  8:00:03 AM: 1.	Changing the bit
a. I purchased 2 different bits, researched and suggested by Gabriel. A Cobalt coated bit, and a Titanium Aluminum Nitride coated bit.
i.	The testing samples showed that although better than our current bit, they were not deemed to be a viable solution.
2.	Changing the descent speed of the drill bit
a.	After removing the drill head guard, we changed the speed, slower by a quarter.
b.	We found that slowing down the descent speed resulted in a tremendous positive difference compared to the original blowout
i.	The original current bit we use now when tested is largely improved and pass qualifications with no issues
1.	Manufacturing was advised to verify with each new order and material that the changed speed continues to have the same qualified results
ii.	The coated bit was observed to be even better than the current bit we currently use. Though better, the change is not necessary. I will keep the bits handy and will document the results.

In the U.S., the common standard to measure rough vacuum is inches of mercury ("Hg), which can be measured in two different ways. One method is as "Hg gauge ("HgV), where the scale starts at 0" Hg (atmospheric pressure) and goes up to 29.92" Hg, which is perfect vacuum.
